title: Philibertia undulata
taxonomy: Philibertia undulata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Apocynaceae, commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. It belongs to the genus Philibertia, which comprises approximately 30 species of flowering plants native to South America. The genus Philibertia is further classified as part of the subfamily Asclepiadoideae. Philibertia undulata is native to the Andes mountain range in South America. This classification is based on botanical research and is subject to revision as new information becomes available.
uses: Philibertia undulata is a plant species that has been used in traditional medicine, particularly in South America. Its medicinal uses include treating various health issues such as fever, cough, and inflammation. The plant's roots and leaves are often used to make infusions or decoctions to relieve these symptoms. Some studies suggest that Philibertia undulata may also have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. However, further research is needed to confirm its effectiveness and safety for human consumption.

constituents: Philibertia undulata contains various active constituents, including alkaloids such as philibertine and philibergine. intro: Philibertia undulata, also known as Devil's Claw, is a perennial plant species native to the Americas, belonging to the family Apocynaceae. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and immunomodulatory effects, providing relief from pain, fever, and digestive issues. The bioactive constituents of Philibertia undulata include harpagoside, harpagide, and harpagide-2', which are responsible for its therapeutic properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Philibertia undulata include capsules, tablets, and teas, often used to treat conditions such as arthritis, back pain, and migraines.
